summary: This post explores how a Codenames player could dominate the game by memorizing the 40 setup cards that come with the game, rather than relying on word associations. The author built a simple bot that uses only positional information from these cards and found it beats most human players, demonstrating that the game has an exploitable side-channel vulnerability. Despite this flaw, the author argues Codenames still has better replayability than most word games, which typically break down after just a few plays when players memorize common word associations.
key_points: A teammate once won a Codenames game by guessing based on physical layout patterns rather than word clues, sparking curiosity about how good someone could be using only position information

A bot that simply memorizes the 40 card configurations and doesn't guess until it can win outright beats most human players, even when humans get unrealistic advantages like 3 perfect guesses per turn
Players subconsciously develop intuitions about valid/invalid patterns after playing enough games, creating a side-channel attack even without deliberate memorization
Most word guessing games break down quickly when played seriously - Codenames is better than most due to combinatorial word associations, though still exploitable
The author used AI assistants (Storytell and Cursor) to write the code for this post, finding them surprisingly capable despite frequent bugs that required iteration
money_quotes: This very silly bot that doesn't guess until it can guess everything is much stronger than most Codenames players.

The relevant question isn't 'when will AI allow laypeople to create better software than programmers?' but 'when will AI allow laypeople to create software that's as good as phone trees and crappy voice recognition are for customer support?'

Most software that's in production today has fairly weak performance and correctness constraints. People basically just accept that software has a ton of bugs and that it's normal to run into hundreds or thousands of software bugs in any given week.
From discussions with founders and VCs in this space, almost everyone has taken this to heart [The Bitter Lesson] - they assume huge improvements are coming and avoid mundane engineering work.
I think people should play games however they find it fun and should find a group that likes playing games in the same way.
key_insight: Even seemingly simple games can have exploitable side-channels that reveal the gap between designed and actual gameplay, much like how AI tools are already 'good enough' for many software tasks despite not being anywhere near perfect.
